# PTP Topology & Multi-Host Scaling

---

## Single Host, One Primary

The simplest case. One WiFi card is the grandmaster; all others are slaves.

```ini
# ptp4l.conf (generated by tsf-sync)
[global]
clockClass              248
priority1               128
priority2               128
domainNumber            42
slaveOnly               0

[/dev/ptp0]                          # primary card — grandmaster
masterOnly              1

[/dev/ptp1]                          # secondary cards — slaves
slaveOnly               1

[/dev/ptp2]
slaveOnly               1

# ... /dev/ptpN
```

---

## Single Host, Upstream PTP Grandmaster

The host receives PTP time from an external source (GPS clock, datacenter PTP). All WiFi cards are slaves.

```
GPS/Atomic ──► NIC PHC ──► ptp4l ──► /dev/ptp0 (Intel WiFi)
                                  ──► /dev/ptp1 (MediaTek WiFi)
                                  ──► /dev/ptpN
```

This is the "datacenter model" — external time source feeds into the PTP domain, and every clock (including WiFi cards) is a slave.

---

## Multi-Host

Each host runs `ptp4l`. One host (or an external device) is the grandmaster. PTP syncs across Ethernet. Within each host, WiFi cards sync to the local PTP domain.

```
              ┌────────────────────────────────┐
              │     PTP Grandmaster             │
              │  (GPS receiver / atomic clock   │
              │   / host with best clock)       │
              └──────────────┬─────────────────┘
                             │ PTP over Ethernet
               ┌─────────────┼─────────────────┐
               │             │                  │
          Host 1          Host 2             Host N
          ptp4l           ptp4l              ptp4l
          ├─/dev/ptp0     ├─/dev/ptp0        ├─/dev/ptp0
          ├─/dev/ptp1     ├─/dev/ptp1        ├─/dev/ptp1
          └─...           └─...              └─...
```

No architectural change from single-host. Same `ptp4l`, different config (add Ethernet port as grandmaster source).

---

## Scaling Properties

| Scale | Architecture | What changes |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 host, 24 cards | One `ptp4l` instance, 24 PTP clocks | Nothing — baseline |
| 1 host, 100 cards | Same `ptp4l` instance | May need to tune poll intervals to reduce bus contention |
| 10 hosts, 100 cards each | `ptp4l` on each host, PTP over Ethernet | Add Ethernet interface to `ptp4l` config |
| 10 hosts, upstream GPS | `ts2phc` feeds GPS into PTP domain | Add `ts2phc` service on grandmaster host |

### Why PTP scales

- **Within a host:** `ptp4l` polls PTP clocks via `/dev/ptpN` ioctls. Each poll is a single `clock_gettime` syscall. The kernel module translates this to one mac80211 `get_tsf` call. 100 clocks = 100 ioctls per sync cycle — trivial for the CPU.

- **Across hosts:** PTP uses standard Ethernet frames (layer 2 or UDP). A switch with IGMP/MLD snooping delivers PTP multicast only to participating hosts. Adding hosts is a configuration change.

- **Clock hierarchy:** PTP's best-master-clock (BMC) algorithm automatically elects the best grandmaster. If the primary host fails, another host can take over.

---

## Network Requirements (Multi-Host)

For multi-host deployments:

- **Switch:** Any managed switch. Hardware timestamping ("PTP-aware" or "transparent clock") switches improve accuracy but aren't required.
- **MLD/IGMP snooping:** Recommended to prevent PTP multicast flooding.
- **Dedicated VLAN:** Optional but recommended to isolate PTP traffic.
- **Latency:** PTP compensates for network delay. On a switched LAN, expect 50-200µs base accuracy, improving to sub-µs with hardware timestamping.
